---
title: "Stimuli"
subtitle: "Give raw media a known shape, timing, and clock before extraction starts."
sidebar: concepts
---

## The problem a stimulus object solves

A NumPy array does not say what it represents. A three-dimensional array might
be one color image, a stack of grayscale video frames, or multichannel audio
arranged incorrectly. It also does not say when the data begins or how quickly
time advances.

A stimulus object adds that missing meaning. It stores the media together with
the facts an extractor needs to interpret it: which axis is time, the sample or
frame rate, the starting time, and the name of the clock used by those times.
It validates those facts immediately, before a feature-computing method can
produce a plausible-looking result from malformed input.

## One wrapper for each kind of input

| Wrapper | Data it holds | Timing it adds |
|---|---|---|
| `ImageStimulus` | One grayscale or color image | An optional onset and duration |
| `VideoStimulus` | A sequence of frames with time on the first axis | Frames per second and a start time |
| `AudioStimulus` | Mono samples or samples-by-channel audio | Samples per second and a start time |
| `TextStimulus` | A Python string | Optional onset and offset arrays when text is already timed |
| `MultiModalStimulus` | Any non-empty combination of image, video, audio, and text | The clock relationships shared by those inputs |

Here is a small example with the four wrappers that each hold one kind of
input:

```{python}
import numpy as np

from natural_features.core.stimulus import (
    AudioStimulus,
    ImageStimulus,
    TextStimulus,
    VideoStimulus,
)

image = ImageStimulus.from_array(
    np.zeros((8, 8, 3)),
    onset_s=0.5,
    duration_s=1.0,
)
video = VideoStimulus.from_array(np.zeros((4, 8, 8, 3)), fps=2.0)
audio = AudioStimulus.from_array(np.zeros(1600), sr_hz=16000)
text = TextStimulus("The scene opens.")

{
    "image_shape": image.image.shape,
    "video_frame_times_s": video.frame_times_s.tolist(),
    "audio_duration_s": audio.samples.shape[0] / audio.sr_hz,
    "text": text.text,
}
```

The four video frames occur at 0.0, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 seconds because the
video rate is two frames per second. The 1,600 audio samples last 0.1 seconds
at 16,000 samples per second.

## What “clock” means here

A clock is a named time origin. The default clock is `stimulus`, where 0
seconds usually means the beginning of the stimulus. If the same recording
began 23 seconds after a scanner run began, stimulus time and scan time are not
the same coordinates. Store that relationship explicitly with a clock map;
do not hide it in `start_offset_s`.

The [Native time and clocks](native-time-and-clocks.qmd) page develops this
example and explains clock conversion step by step.

## What is checked immediately

Images must be numeric, non-empty, and shaped as height-by-width with an
optional color-channel axis. Image values are converted to floating point and
clipped to the range from 0 to 1. Videos must contain at least one frame and
have a positive frame rate. Audio must contain at least one sample, have one or
two dimensions, and have a positive sample rate. Text must be a Python string.

`MultiModalStimulus` must contain at least one modality. When its components
carry clock mappings, those mappings must agree. A contradiction is reported
when the object is built, not deferred until alignment.

## Where the wrapper stops

A stimulus wrapper describes media already in memory or loaded by one of its
specific constructors. It is not a universal media decoder. High-level
workflows handle path coercion and broader media preparation. The wrapper also
does not align a stimulus to a scanner merely because both have numeric start
times; that requires an explicit clock map.

## See also

[Wrap a stimulus](../start/01-wrap-a-stimulus.qmd) · [Feature objects](feature-objects.qmd) · [Feature object schemas](../spec/feature-object-schemas.qmd)
